title: Self-Hosting: When It’s Worth It (and When It’s Not)
excerpt: Self-hosting gives control and privacy, but comes with real maintenance costs.
content:
Self-hosting has a certain appeal if you're technically inclined. Full control, no subscription fees, no third party reading your data. But it also has real costs that don't always make it into the enthusiasm of the initial setup.

Here's an honest look at when it makes sense — and when it doesn't.

What self-hosting actually means

You're running software on hardware you control — usually a home server or a cheap VPS. That means you're responsible for keeping it running, keeping it secure, backing it up, and fixing it when something breaks at an inconvenient time.

The "free" in self-hosting refers to money, not time.

When it's worth it

You care about data privacy for a specific use case. Running your own Nextcloud instance means your documents never leave infrastructure you control. For sensitive professional or personal data, that's a meaningful difference from handing it to a cloud provider.

You enjoy the process. There's genuine satisfaction in getting a stack running cleanly — choosing the right services, wiring them together, watching them work. If the setup and occasional debugging feel like interesting problems rather than chores, self-hosting rewards that mindset well.

You have the technical baseline. Comfortable with Linux, networking basics, and reading logs? Self-hosting is approachable. If those things are new to you, it will teach them — but expect a bumpy start.

When it's not worth it

When reliability matters. Hosted services have redundancy and teams dedicated to uptime. Your home server does not. A power cut, a failed drive, or an expired certificate will take things down at the worst possible moment.

When you're already stretched thin. Self-hosted systems need occasional attention — updates, certificate renewals, storage management. If your time is already limited, that overhead accumulates and systems tend to drift into a neglected, half-broken state.

When a good hosted alternative exists. Email is the clearest example. The security burden of running your own mail server is high, and the failure modes are painful. Services like Proton give you meaningful privacy benefits without the operational complexity.

The practical middle ground

You don't have to go all-in. Self-hosting one or two things — a notes app, a media server, a password manager — lets you get the benefits where they matter most without committing to a full homelab.

Start with something low-stakes. Learn how it breaks. Then decide if you want more.

title: European Alternatives to Big Tech
excerpt: A practical look at European tools that prioritize privacy and control over data.
content:
The convenience of Big Tech is real — but so is the cost. Your data funds advertising systems you have no visibility into. The question isn't whether to care, but what to actually do about it.

Here are some alternatives I've used or looked into. Not all are European, but all are worth knowing about.

Proton — the clearest win

Proton is Swiss, open-source, and built around privacy by design. I've been using Proton Mail and the experience is genuinely good. The interface is clean, search works, and you're not being profiled. It's not a downgrade — it just works.

Proton also offers a VPN, calendar, drive, and password manager, all under one account. If you want to move off Google's ecosystem incrementally, Proton is probably the best starting point. The free tier has storage limits, but the paid plan is reasonably priced for what you get.

Nothing — not European, but not Big Tech either

Nothing is a British startup making phones and accessories that push back against the bloated, ad-heavy Android experience. I have the Nothing Phone and the Nothing Watch, and both have earned a permanent place in my setup.

The phone runs a clean version of Android with almost no pre-installed junk. It's fast, genuinely affordable, and the transparent back with LED glyphs is fun without being gimmicky. But beyond the aesthetics, it just works without getting in the way.

The watch surprised me more. Most smartwatches try to do everything and end up doing most things poorly. The Nothing Watch is focused — notifications, activity tracking, days of battery — without the bloat. I barely think about it, which is exactly the point.

Other tools worth knowing

  • Nextcloud — self-hosted file storage and collaboration. More setup, but full control over your data
  • Fastmail — Australian, privacy-focused email if Proton doesn't fit your workflow
  • Ecosia — German search engine. Not as strong on technical queries, but fine for everyday use
  • Signal — messaging with proper end-to-end encryption, open-source and audited

You don't need to switch everything at once

Start with one thing. Email is a good first step — it's where a lot of sensitive information lives and it's one of the easier swaps. Get comfortable, then move to the next tool.

The goal isn't to live off-grid. It's to make deliberate choices about where your data goes.

title: Building Websites with AI is Faster Than You Expect
excerpt: AI changes how websites are built—faster iteration, tighter feedback loops, and more experimentation.
content:
I built this website with AI assistance. Not just boilerplate — a full static site with a custom orbital simulation as the hero background, a radar mini-game gating the CV, a blog with automated weekly scheduling, and a Notion-to-JSON sync pipeline. Things that would have taken weeks of evening sessions took days.

Here's what I actually learned from the process.

The old way

A few years ago, building a personal site from scratch meant choosing between learning a framework properly or stitching together tutorials. Either way, you'd spend as much time fighting tooling as actually building. Webpack configs, CSS specificity wars, an afternoon lost to a Canvas DPI bug with nothing to show for it.

What actually changed

AI tools don't eliminate the hard parts — they compress them dramatically. You describe what you want, get a working first draft quickly, and when something breaks, you explain the symptom and get a targeted fix instead of sifting through documentation and forum posts.

The real shift is in how fast the feedback loop becomes. Instead of planning everything upfront to avoid costly mistakes, you try things. Doesn't work? Iterate. Want to add eight orbiting objects — satellites, a saxophone, a Game Boy — as a full-viewport canvas background? Sure, try it. The experimentation cost is low enough that it becomes fun again.

What it doesn't replace

Understanding still matters. When the canvas kept rendering in the wrong position, I needed to know enough about how browsers handle intrinsic element sizing versus CSS-controlled sizing to describe the problem clearly. The AI gave me the fix — but I had to know what to ask.

You also have to review what gets generated. Not everything is right. Edge cases get missed, and sometimes the approach is technically correct but wrong for your specific situation. The output is a starting point, not a final answer.

The honest summary

AI-assisted development feels like working with a fast, knowledgeable collaborator who never gets tired. It's not a replacement for understanding how things work — it's an accelerant. The more context you bring, the better the output.

If you've been putting off a project because the ramp-up felt too steep, the ramp is genuinely shorter than it used to be. Start small and see where it takes you.

title: Minimalism in Tech (Fewer Tools, Better Workflow)
excerpt: Using fewer, better tools reduces friction and improves focus.
content:
At some point you look at your setup and realise you have tools for your tools. An app to organise your apps. A productivity system to manage your productivity system. The overhead became the work.

Pulling back has been more useful than adding.

How tool accumulation happens

It starts innocuously. You try a new note-taking app because it has a feature your current one doesn't. You add a browser extension to fix something small. You sign up for a service that solves a specific problem. Each decision makes local sense.

But tools have hidden maintenance costs. You have to remember they exist. They need updates. They create habits and expectations. The more you have, the more cognitive load you're carrying before you've done anything useful.

What fewer tools actually looks like

I use a Nothing Phone. The decision was partly price — it's genuinely affordable — but the experience reinforced the philosophy. It runs clean Android with almost no pre-installed apps, no algorithmic feed, no bloatware demanding attention. It's a phone that stays out of the way.

The Nothing Watch takes it further. One screen. Notifications, activity, time. No app store, no social features, battery that lasts days. I stopped thinking about it almost immediately — which is exactly the point. A tool you don't have to think about is a good tool.

The principle underneath

Every tool you add is a choice about where your attention will go. Fewer choices means more focus on the things that matter. That's not about being ascetic — it's about signal-to-noise ratio.

The same applies to software stacks and development environments. The most effective engineers I've worked with tend to have deep familiarity with a small set of tools, not surface familiarity with many.

How to simplify without losing capability

Don't remove tools randomly — remove duplication and habit. Ask: is this doing something nothing else does? Am I using it regularly? If not, cut it.

Then notice what you don't miss. That's usually more than you expected.

You gain focus, not limitation

The goal isn't to have less. It's to have what actually matters, working well, without friction. That's a harder bar to meet than just adding more — but it's the one worth optimising for.

title: Why Most Side Projects Fail (and How to Avoid It)
excerpt: Side projects fail due to scope, inconsistency, and lack of completion—not bad ideas.
content:
Most side projects don't fail because the idea was bad. They fail because of scope creep, inconsistency, and the gap between starting something and finishing it.

I've been on both sides of this — projects that quietly died and one that ended up in orbit.

The pattern that kills projects

It usually starts well. The initial idea is exciting, the first session is productive, and you can already picture the finished thing. Then real life intervenes. You miss a week. The context is gone, and picking it back up feels harder than starting something new.

Or it grows. What started as a simple script becomes a full application. What was a quick prototype turns into a platform. Each addition feels logical in isolation, but collectively they push the finish line back until there isn't one.

What constraint actually does

The clearest lesson from projects that shipped: a narrow scope forces decisions. When you're building a CubeSat subsystem at a student organisation, you can't keep adding requirements — mass and power budgets are real and unforgiving. That constraint is clarifying, not limiting. The same principle applies to any project.

Define what done looks like before you start. Not "a web app" but "a single page that does X and can be shown to someone." If you can't answer that clearly, the project doesn't have a shape yet — and shapeless projects drift.

Consistency beats motivation

Motivation is high at the start and unreliable after that. A fixed slot each week — even 45 minutes — beats sporadic full-day sessions. You maintain context, the work stays alive in your head, and progress compounds.

Treat showing up as the skill, not the output of any given session.

Separate building from polishing

Done beats perfect. Get to a working state as fast as possible, even if it's rough. A working thing can be improved and shown to people. A half-built thing usually can't be shipped at all, and the longer it sits unfinished, the harder it is to return to.

Small and complete beats big and abandoned

The value of a finished small project is higher than an unfinished ambitious one. A complete, working thing shows you can ship. An elaborate half-finished thing shows you started — and that's a much harder sell, to yourself or anyone else.

Finish something. Then decide if it should grow.

title: Why Playing an Instrument for Years Changes You
excerpt: Playing an instrument long-term reshapes how you think, connect, and express yourself.
content:
Sixteen years with a saxophone stops being a hobby and starts becoming part of how you think.

The beginning is deceptive

When you first pick up an instrument, it's all mechanics. Notes, scales, where to put your fingers. You're translating dots on a page into sound, and it feels like learning a language by memorising vocabulary without any grammar.

For the first few years, progress is fast and visible. Every week you can do something you couldn't do before. Then it plateaus. That's where most people stop — but it's also where the real learning begins.

It stops being about the notes

At some point, probably without noticing, you stop thinking about what you're playing and start thinking about how and why. The technique becomes background noise. What's left is timing, dynamics, expression. You start hearing things in music you never noticed before — the space between notes, the way a phrase breathes, why a certain rhythm feels urgent.

Playing with other people accelerates this. Jazz especially. In a jam session, you're constantly listening and responding in real time — there's no pause button. You learn to communicate without words, to follow someone else's idea and build on it. That skill transfers.

The discipline it quietly installs

An instrument shows you, concretely, what consistent practice does. Not motivation — practice. You learn that 20 minutes five times a week beats a three-hour session once a month. You get better at breaking a hard problem into smaller parts, at being patient with slow progress.

This turned out to be useful everywhere else. Engineering projects, studying, learning new tools — the mindset is the same. Small reps, compounding over time.

The emotional side

There's something about music that bypasses language. On a bad day, 30 minutes of playing resets something that talking doesn't. I don't fully understand why. It just does.

You also get an unexpected social connector. Musicians find each other. Shared repertoire creates instant common ground with strangers in a way most other hobbies don't.

You don't need decades to benefit

The benefits aren't locked behind years of practice — they start early. Start with whatever instrument you're drawn to, commit to 15 minutes a day, and let the rest follow.

Just start.

title: Six months at ESA ESOC: what I learned
excerpt: From coffee-fuelled orbit determination scripts to CDRs with real stakes — here's what surprised me most.
content:
Six months at ESA ESOC flew by faster than an OPS-SAT pass over Darmstadt. Here's what stuck with me.

The pace of real ops is different

Student satellite projects have generous margins — you can iterate, rebuild, re-solder. Operational missions are different. The stakes are real: wrong commanding, wrong code, wrong timeline can mean a lost experiment or a damaged asset. Working inside that environment changed how I think about reliability and verification.

Orbit determination is harder than the textbooks suggest

I spent weeks building a precise orbit determination algorithm from GNSS data. In principle, it's "just" least squares with some orbital mechanics. In practice, you're wrestling with measurement noise, clock biases, atmospheric modelling, and data gaps. The final pipeline worked — but I understood deeply why the research literature on this topic is so thick.

The people make the place

ESOC is genuinely multinational. In any given meeting you might hear four languages in the hallway before it starts. That kind of environment makes you sharper — you have to communicate precisely because assumptions don't always travel well across borders.

What's next

I'm back in Trondheim finishing my thesis, but the ESA internship confirmed that space operations and systems engineering is where I want to build a career. Next stop: graduation. Then: wherever the best mission is.

title: Why I think every engineer should try PCB design
excerpt: You don't have to be a hardware person to benefit from designing a circuit board.
content:
Most software engineers I know have never laid out a PCB. I think that's a shame — not because everyone needs to become a hardware engineer, but because the constraints of physical design make you a better thinker.

The discipline of physical constraints

When you write software, abstraction layers hide an enormous amount of complexity. Memory is just there. Signals propagate instantly. In PCB design, none of this is true. You have to think about current return paths, trace impedance, thermal dissipation, and manufacturing tolerances all at once. That concreteness is clarifying.

The cost of a mistake is real

A wrong diode footprint means waiting three weeks for a re-spin. That changes how carefully you review your work. I've become a much better checker of my own work since I started designing PCBs — because I know what shipping a mistake costs.

Start somewhere simple

You don't need to design a satellite OBC on your first board. Pick a project: a custom USB-C power delivery adapter, a sensor breakout, an LED controller. Use KiCad (free, excellent), order from JLCPCB, and iterate. The first board will have errors. That's fine. The second will be better.

The best engineers I know understand hardware even if they don't do it every day. Give it a try.
